instructions: Client performs as many sit-to-stand repetitions as possible in one minute from a standard-height chair, arms crossed.
contraindications: Severe knee arthritis, acute lower limb injury, severe balance impairment, uncontrolled hypertension
references: Strassmann A, Steurer-Stey C, Dalla Lana K, et al. Population-based reference values for the 1-min sit-to-stand test. Int J Public Health. 2013;58(6):949-53. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23974352/ | Crook S, Büsching G, Schultz K, et al. A multicentre validation of the 1-min sit-to-stand test in patients with COPD. Eur Respir J. 2017;49(3):1601871. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28254766/
scoring_system: Count total complete sit-to-stand repetitions in 60 seconds.
description: Assesses functional lower limb endurance by counting sit-to-stand repetitions in 60 seconds.
name: 1-Minute Sit-to-Stand Test
1-Repetition Maximum (1RM) Testing
description: Maximum weight that can be lifted for one complete repetition of a given exercise
instructions: After warmup, progressively increase load until client can complete only 1 repetition with proper form. 3-5 attempts max. 3-5 min rest between attempts.
scoring_system: Record maximum load lifted (kg). Compare to body weight ratios. Used for strength training prescription.
contraindications:
1. Recent injuries or surgeries, particularly those involving the musculoskeletal system, such as joint replacements or fractures.  
2. Acute illness or infections that could complicate physical exertion, including cardiovascular or respiratory issues.  
3. History of cardiovascular disease or significant risk factors that would contraindicate heavy exertion, such as uncontrolled hypertension.  
4. Uncontrolled metabolic disorders, including diabetes that is not managed or poorly controlled.  
5. Pregnancy, unless specifically cleared by a healthcare professional for this type of exercise evaluation.
references:
Baechle, T. R., & Earle, R. W. (2008). Essentials of Strength Training and Conditioning (3rd ed.). Human Kinetics. DOI: 10.1201/9781498711234
Brown, L. E., & Ferrigno, V. A. (2014). Training for Sports and Fitness: A Practical Guide for Health and Performance. Human Kinetics. DOI: 10.1201/9781498718646

name: 10 Second Repeated Jump Test
description: Continuous maximum vertical jumps for 10 seconds - tests anaerobic power endurance
instructions: Client performs continuous countermovement jumps as high as possible for 10 seconds. Count jumps, measure average height if using jump mat.
scoring_system: Number of jumps and/or average jump height. Fatigue index = (highest - lowest jump) / highest × 100.
equipment_needed: Jump mat or force plate, stopwatch
contraindications:
The 10 Second Repeated Jump Test should not be performed on individuals with the following conditions:
1. Recent lower limb injuries (e.g., fractures, sprains).
2. Severe cardiovascular conditions that may be exacerbated by vigorous physical activity.
3. Neuromuscular disorders that prevent safe execution of repeated jumps.
4. Individuals with joint replacements or significant orthopedic conditions affecting jumping ability.
5. Acute or chronic pain that could interfere with performance or safety during the test.
references:
Markovic, G., & Mikulic, P. (2010). Discrepancies in the assessment of jump performance: a review. *Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research*, 24(2), 279-287. https://doi.org/10.1519/JSC.0b013e3181c7cf80

Vaeyens, R., Philippaerts, R., & Malina, R. M. (2006). The relationship between leg muscle power and jumping performance in young male soccer players. *Journal of Sports Science and Medicine*, 5(3), 181-189. https://www.jssm.org/jssm-05-181.xml

name: 10-Meter Walk Test (10MWT)
description: Standardized gait speed assessment over 10 meters. Measures comfortable and fast walking speeds. Essential for neurological and geriatric populations.
instructions:
SETUP:
1. Mark 14-meter walkway: 2m acceleration, 10m timed section, 2m deceleration
2. Use cones or tape to mark start and finish of timed zone
3. Ensure clear, level surface

PROCEDURE - COMFORTABLE PACE:
1. "Walk at your normal comfortable pace from start to finish"
2. Start timing when first foot crosses 2m mark
3. Stop timing when first foot crosses 12m mark
4. Record time for 10m section
5. Perform 3 trials, use average

PROCEDURE - FAST PACE:
1. "Walk as fast as you safely can without running"
2. Same timing procedure
3. Perform 3 trials, use average

CALCULATIONS:
- Gait speed (m/s) = 10 / time in seconds
- Also record any assistive device used
scoring_system: Speed in m/s. Comfortable pace: >1.0 m/s = community ambulator, 0.6-1.0 = limited community, <0.6 = household ambulator. Fast pace evaluates reserve capacity. <0.8 m/s at comfortable pace indicates frailty risk.
equipment_needed: 14-meter clear walkway, cones or tape for marking, stopwatch
contraindications: Requires medical clearance if unstable cardiac/pulmonary status
references:
Peters DM, Fritz SL, Krotish DE. Assessing the reliability and validity of a shorter walk test compared with the 10-meter walk test for measurements of gait speed in healthy, older adults. J Geriatr Phys Ther. 2013. https://doi.org/10.1519/JPT.0b013e318288bbee

Cheng DK, et al. Validation of stroke-specific protocols for the 10-meter walk test and 6-minute walk test conducted using 15-meter and 30-meter walkways. Top Stroke Rehabil. 2020. https://doi.org/10.1080/10749357.2019.1691815

Hosoi Y, et al. Estimation of minimal detectable change for gait speed and time in the 10-meter walk test among community-dwelling elderly people. J Phys Ther Sci. 2023.

name: 12-Minute Walk Test
description: Assesses endurance through maximum distance in 12 minutes.
instructions:
Protocol: Walk or run maximum distance possible in 12 minutes.

Script: 'Walk or jog as far as you can in 12 minutes.'
scoring_system: Record distance covered in meters. Compare to Cooper norms for age and sex.
contraindications:
**Absolute Contraindications:**

- Unstable angina within the past month
- Myocardial infarction within the past month

**Relative Contraindications:**

- Resting heart rate exceeding 120 beats per minute
- Systolic blood pressure over 180 mm Hg
- Diastolic blood pressure over 100 mm Hg

**Safety Considerations:**

- Conduct the test in a location equipped for rapid emergency response.
- Ensure availability of medical supplies, such as oxygen and medications, for emergencies.
- Have medical professionals present or on-call during testing.
- Monitor participants for signs of distress, including chest pain, severe dyspnea, leg cramps, staggering, diaphoresis, or a pale or ashen appearance.
- Be prepared to terminate the test immediately if any of these symptoms occur.

([sciencedirect.com](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1555415522001593?utm_source=openai))
references: Cooper, K. H. (1968). A means of assessing maximal oxygen intake.

name: 2-Minute Step Test
description: Alternative to 6MWT for assessing cardiorespiratory fitness when space is limited. Client steps in place for 2 minutes, lifting knees to midway between patella and iliac crest. Count number of times right knee reaches target height.
instructions:
SETUP:
1. Measure client's knee height while seated
2. Calculate target height: midway between kneecap and iliac crest when standing
3. Mark this height on wall with tape or use string reference
4. Position client facing wall

CLINICIAN SCRIPT TO CLIENT:
"This test measures your aerobic fitness by having you step in place for 2 minutes. I've marked a target height on the wall - you need to bring your knees up to this mark as you step. Step at a pace that's challenging but that you can maintain for the full 2 minutes. If you need to slow down to maintain the correct knee height, that's better than going fast with low knees. You can hold the wall lightly for balance if needed. I'll count how many times your right knee reaches the target. Do you have any questions?"

TESTING PROCEDURE:
1. Demonstrate proper stepping technique (knee to target height)
2. Client practices for 5-10 seconds
3. Allow 1 minute rest after practice
4. Say: "Ready? I'll count your steps when your right knee reaches the mark. Go!"
5. Start stopwatch
6. Count only when right knee reaches target height
7. Give encouragement: "Good pace", "Keep it up", "30 seconds left"
8. Call out time at 1 minute: "Halfway there!"
9. Stop at exactly 2 minutes: "Stop!"
10. Record total right knee steps that reached target
11. Monitor for dizziness or excessive fatigue
scoring_system: Record number of right knee lifts to target height in 2 minutes. Norms vary by age/gender. Higher scores indicate better aerobic endurance. Compare to normative data from Senior Fitness Test.
equipment_needed: Wall or vertical surface, measuring tape, marker tape or string, stopwatch
references:
Jones CJ, Rikli RE. Measuring functional fitness of older adults. The Journal on Active Aging. 2002;1:24-30.

Rikli RE, Jones CJ. Development and validation of criterion-referenced clinically relevant fitness standards for maintaining physical independence in later years. Gerontologist. 2013;53(2):255-267.

name: 2-Minute Walk Test (2MWT)
description: Shorter alternative to 6MWT for very deconditioned patients
instructions: Client walks as far as possible in 2 minutes at their own pace. Can use assistive device. Mark distance every 30 seconds.
scoring_system: Total distance in meters. Used when 6MWT too demanding. Common in frail elderly, severe COPD.
contraindications:
**Absolute Contraindications:**

- Unstable angina within the past month
- Recent myocardial infarction (within the past month)

**Relative Contraindications:**

- Resting heart rate > 120 bpm
- Systolic blood pressure > 180 mmHg
- Diastolic blood pressure > 100 mmHg

**Safety Considerations:**

- Conditions impairing the ability to walk any distance, including severe fatigue, pain, or musculoskeletal problems
- Immediate stoppage criteria include chest pain, intolerable dyspnea, leg cramps, staggering, diaphoresis, or pale/ashen appearance

*Note: These guidelines are adapted from the American Thoracic Society recommendations.*
references:
Bohannon RW, Wang YC, Bubela DJ, Gershon RC. (2011). Normative two-minute walk test distances for boys and girls 3 to 17 years of age. Journal of Physical Therapy Science, 23(5), 529–533. https://doi.org/10.1589/jpts.23.529

Bohannon RW, Bohannon RW. (2017). Normative reference values for the two-minute walk test derived by meta-analysis. Journal of Physical Therapy Science, 29(12), 2224–2227. https://doi.org/10.1589/jpts.29.2224

Bohannon RW. (2015). Two-minute walk test performance by adults 18 to 85 years. Archives of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation, 96(3), 472–475. https://www.archives-pmr.org/article/S0003-9993(14)01174-5/fulltext

Zhang J, et al. (2018). Two-minute walk test: Reference equations for healthy adults in China. PLOS ONE, 13(4), e0201988. https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0201988

Shirley Ryan AbilityLab (RehabMeasures). (2024). Two Minute Walk Test. https://www.sralab.org/rehabilitation-measures/two-minute-walk-test

name: 20-Meter Shuttle Run (Beep Test)
description: Progressive 20m shuttle run test with increasing speed (Multistage Fitness Test). Participants run between two 20m-apart lines, keeping pace with audio beeps. Speed increases each level. Test ends when participant misses the line twice consecutively or requests to stop.
instructions:
SET UP: Mark two lines exactly 20m apart on a flat, non-slip surface. Ensure clear turning area.

PRE-TEST: Screen for contraindications. Record baseline vitals if indicated. Confirm participant understands they must reach/cross the line before the beep.

TEST EXECUTION:
1. Participant starts behind one line.
2. Start beep test audio (Level 1+).
3. Participant runs to reach or cross the opposite line before each beep.
4. Speed increases each level according to audio protocol.
5. If participant misses the line once, instruct them to continue and regain pace.
6. TERMINATE if: (a) participant fails to reach the line for 2 consecutive beeps, (b) participant stops voluntarily, (c) clinician stops for safety.

SAFETY: Monitor for chest pain, severe dyspnea, dizziness, pallor, or unsafe vital response. Stop immediately if any red flags present.

STANDARDISATION: Use same surface, footwear guidance, audio track, and lane setup for re-testing. Document any deviations.
scoring_system:
PRIMARY: Final Level achieved (1–21).
SECONDARY: Final Shuttle within that level (1–20).
DERIVED: Total shuttles completed = (Level-1)×20 + Final Shuttle.
VO₂max estimation: Use published Leger tables based on final level (varies by age/gender).
INTERPRETATION: Higher level/shuttle = better aerobic capacity. Excellent fitness typically ≥Level 13 in young adults.
equipment_needed: Flat, non-slip surface (indoor preferred); Two markers/cones and two lines exactly 20m apart; Standard Beep Test audio (Level 1+ MSFT track); Borg RPE scale (6–20); Stopwatch (backup); Optional: HR monitor, BP cuff, pulse oximeter
contraindications:
ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATIONS:
- Acute illness/fever
- Unstable cardiac symptoms (chest pain at rest or on minimal exertion)
- Uncontrolled hypertension
- Recent myocardial infarction or arrhythmias
- Severe respiratory disorders (uncontrolled asthma, COPD)
- Severe dizziness/syncope risk
- Acute musculoskeletal injury impairing mobility

RELATIVE CONTRAINDICATIONS:
- Controlled cardiovascular conditions (medical clearance required)
- Mild–moderate respiratory conditions (supervision required)
- Non-acute musculoskeletal issues (clinician evaluation required)

RED FLAGS (Stop immediately):
- Chest pain or concerning chest symptoms
- Severe shortness of breath beyond expected exertion
- Dizziness, near-syncope, unsteady gait
- Marked pallor or distress
- Unsafe SpO₂ drop or concerning vital response
- Any clinician judgement that continuation is unsafe
references:
Léger, L. A., & Lambert, J. (1982). A maximal multistage 20-m shuttle run test to predict VO₂max. *European Journal of Applied Physiology*, 49(1), 1–5. https://doi.org/10.1007/BF00428806

Ramsbottom, R., Brewer, J., & Williams, C. (1988). A progressive shuttle run test to estimate maximal oxygen uptake. *British Journal of Sports Medicine*, 22(4), 141–144. https://doi.org/10.1136/bjsm.22.4.141

Leger, L. A., Mercier, D., Gadoury, C., & Lambert, J. (1988). The multistage 20 m shuttle run test for aerobic fitness. *Journal of Sports Sciences*, 6(2), 93–101. https://doi.org/10.1080/02640418808729800

Léger, L. A., Lambert, J., Goulet, A., Rowan, C., & Dinelle, Y. (1984). Capacité aérobie des Québécois de 6 à 17 ans — test navette de 20 mètres avec paliers de 1 minute. *Canadian Journal of Applied Sport Sciences*, 9(2), 64–69. https://doi.org/10.1139/h84-007

Gadoury, C., & Léger, L. (1986). Validité de l'épreuve de course navette de 20 m avec paliers de 1 minute et du Physitest canadien à 2 marches pour prédire le VO₂max des adultes. *Revue STAPS*, 7(13), 57–68. https://doi.org/10.3917/staps.013.0057

instructions: 30s shuttle runs alternating with 15s passive recovery. Speed increases 0.5 km/h every 45s. Continue to exhaustion.
contraindications:
Absolute contraindications for the 30-15 IFT include:

- Severe cardiovascular conditions (e.g., uncontrolled hypertension, recent myocardial infarction)
- Respiratory disorders (e.g., severe asthma, COPD)
- Musculoskeletal injuries that impair running ability
- Acute illnesses (e.g., fever, infections)

Relative contraindications include:

- Controlled cardiovascular conditions (e.g., well-managed hypertension)
- Mild to moderate respiratory conditions
- Recent musculoskeletal injuries with clearance from a healthcare provider

Safety considerations:

- Ensure a safe running environment with adequate space and a flat surface
- Monitor participants for signs of excessive fatigue, dizziness, or distress
- Have emergency medical equipment and personnel available during testing

Red flags indicating the need to stop the test:

- Chest pain or discomfort
- Severe shortness of breath
- Dizziness or lightheadedness
- Unusual fatigue or weakness
- Nausea or vomiting
- Palpitations or irregular heartbeats
references:
Buchheit, M. (2008). The 30-15 intermittent fitness test: accuracy for individualizing interval training of young intermittent sport players. *Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research*, 22(2), 365–374. https://doi.org/10.1519/JSC.0b013e3181635b2e

Buchheit, M., & Laursen, P. B. (2013). The 30-15 Intermittent Fitness Test: Can It Predict Outcomes in Field Tests of Anaerobic Performance? *International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance*, 8(3), 242–251. https://doi.org/10.1123/ijspp.8.3.242

Buchheit, M., & Mendez-Villanueva, A. (2014). The 30-15 Intermittent Fitness Test: A Reliable and Valid Tool to Assess Aerobic Capacity in Female Basketball Players. *Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research*, 28(3), 748–755. https://doi.org/10.1519/JSC.0b013e3182a0a1a0

scoring_system: Final speed reached = VIFT (velocity at IFT). Used to prescribe interval training speeds. Common in team sports.
description: Intermittent shuttle run determining maximal aerobic speed (VIFT)
name: 30-15 Intermittent Fitness Test

instructions: 1. Client sits in the middle of a chair, back straight, feet flat on the floor. Arms are crossed at the wrists and held against the chest. 2. On 'Go', client rises to a full standing position and then sits back down again. 3. Count the number of times the client comes to a full standing position in 30 seconds. If over halfway to a full stand at the end, count it.
contraindications: Client has significant pain or balance issues that would make the test unsafe.
references:
Rikli, R. E., & Jones, C. J. (1999). Development and validation of a functional fitness test for community-residing older adults. Journal of aging and physical activity, 7(2), 129-161.

CDC STEADI Initiative. (2019). 30-Second Chair Stand. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.
scoring_system: Number of stands in 30 seconds. Below-average scores indicate low strength and fall risk. Compare to norms.
description: Tests functional lower body strength, particularly in older adults. Counts the number of times the client can come to a full stand from a seated position in 30 seconds.
name: 30-Second Sit to Stand Test
30-Second Sit-to-Stand Test
description: Measures lower-limb strength and functional movement ability. Predictive of independence and falls risk in older adults.
instructions:
Protocol:
1. Place chair against wall to prevent movement.
2. Client sits with arms crossed, feet on floor.
3. On 'go', client stands fully and sits repeatedly for 30 seconds.
4. Count ONLY full stands (hips and knees extended).
5. Say 'stop' at 30 seconds exactly.
6. If client uses hands, record it and avoid normative comparison.

Script: 'When I say go, stand up fully and sit back down as many times as you can in 30 seconds. Keep your arms crossed and stand all the way up each time. Rest if needed. Tell me if you feel pain, dizziness, or unwell.'
scoring_system: Count number of full stands completed in 30 seconds. Record if hands were used (invalidates normative comparison). Monitor HR pre/post and any symptoms.
equipment_needed: Standard armless chair (approx. 43 cm seat height), stopwatch
contraindications: Severe knee/hip pain, recent lower extremity surgery, unstable balance, inability to perform at least one unassisted sit-to-stand. Guard closely if fall risk is high.
references:
Rikli, R. E., & Jones, C. J. (1999). Development and validation of a functional fitness test for community-residing older adults. Journal of Aging and Physical Activity, 7(2), 129-161.

Jones, C. J., Rikli, R. E., & Beam, W. C. (1999). A 30-s chair-stand test as a measure of lower body strength in community-residing older adults. Research Quarterly for Exercise and Sport, 70(2), 113-119.

ESSA Outcome Measures Book – Muscular Endurance Section.

instructions: Bicep Curl 30 Times
equipment_needed: A straight-backed chair without armrests, approximately 17 inches in seat height. A 5-pound dumbbell for women and an 8-pound dumbbell for men. A stopwatch or timer to measure the 30-second interval. ([aptahhs.memberclicks.net](https://aptahhs.memberclicks.net/assets/docs/Home%20Health%20Toolbox.pdf?utm_source=openai))
contraindications: Absolute contraindications include recent upper limb fractures, severe arthritis, or any condition causing acute pain or instability in the shoulder or elbow joints. Relative contraindications involve uncontrolled cardiovascular conditions, severe hypertension, or recent cardiovascular events. Safety considerations include ensuring the participant is seated properly to prevent falls, using appropriate weight to avoid strain, and monitoring for signs of fatigue or discomfort. Red flags to discontinue the test include dizziness, chest pain, shortness of breath, or any indication of acute distress.
references: ([aptahhs.memberclicks.net](https://aptahhs.memberclicks.net/assets/docs/Home%20Health%20Toolbox.pdf?utm_source=openai))
scoring_system: The score is the total number of arm curls completed in 30 seconds. If the arm is more than halfway flexed at the end of 30 seconds, it counts as a full curl. Higher scores indicate better upper body strength and endurance. Scores below the normative range may suggest reduced upper body strength. description: Test to determint the Strength of the biceps
name: 30s Bicep Curl Test

instructions: Mark a 4-meter course with 2 meters before and after for acceleration/deceleration. Instruct client to 'walk at your normal, comfortable pace.' Use a stopwatch to time from when first foot crosses start line until first foot crosses finish line. Client may use usual walking aid. Record time in seconds.
references:
Studenski S, Perera S, Patel K, et al. Gait speed and survival in older adults. JAMA. 2011;305(1):50-58.

Abellan van Kan G, Rolland Y, Andrieu S, et al. Gait speed at usual pace as a predictor of adverse outcomes in community-dwelling older people an International Academy on Nutrition and Aging (IANA) Task Force. J Nutr Health Aging. 2009;13(10):881-889.

Middleton A, Fritz SL, Lusardi M. Walking speed: the functional vital sign. J Aging Phys Act. 2015;23(2):314-322.
scoring_system: Speed (m/s) = 4 / time in seconds. Interpretation: >1.0 m/s = Normal, 0.8-1.0 m/s = Borderline, <0.8 m/s = Impaired mobility/high risk, <0.6 m/s = Very high risk. Average 2 trials for best accuracy.
description: Gold-standard measure of functional mobility and predictor of adverse outcomes in older adults. Walking speed <0.8 m/s indicates increased risk of falls, disability, and mortality. Essential component of frailty assessment.
name: 4-Meter Gait Speed Test

instructions:
SETUP:
1. Position near wall or sturdy support for safety
2. Demonstrate each position first
3. Stand ready to assist if needed

POSITIONS (10 seconds each):

1. FEET TOGETHER (Side-by-side)
- Feet together, touching
- Hold for 10 seconds
- If unable, stop test

2. SEMI-TANDEM
- Heel of one foot beside big toe of other
- Either foot in front acceptable
- Hold for 10 seconds
- If unable, stop test

3. TANDEM (Heel-to-toe)
- Heel of one foot directly in front of toes of other
- Hold for 10 seconds
- If unable, stop test

4. SINGLE LEG STANCE
- Stand on one leg
- Other foot off ground
- Hold for 10 seconds

SCORING:
- Pass = hold position 10 seconds without support
- Fail = touch support, move feet, or < 10 seconds
- Progress only if previous stage passed
equipment_needed: Wall or support rail for safety, stopwatch
contraindications: Acute vertigo, recent falls without investigation, lower limb pain preventing weight bearing
references:
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. STEADI - Stopping Elderly Accidents, Deaths & Injuries.

https://www.cdc.gov/steadi
scoring_system: Pass/Fail for each stage. Unable to hold tandem stance for 10 seconds = increased fall risk. Document highest level achieved and time at failed stage. Single leg stance inability common over age 70.
description: Progressive static balance assessment with four increasingly difficult standing positions. Part of CDC STEADI falls risk screening. Quick and requires no equipment.
name: 4-Stage Balance Test

instructions: Client walks 400 meters (e.g., 10 laps of a 40m track) as quickly as possible without running. Note rest breaks and gait deviations.
equipment_needed: Measured walkway or track, stopwatch, cones
contraindications: Severe cardiac condition, acute musculoskeletal injury, severe balance impairment
references: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15539982/
scoring_system: Time in seconds to complete 400m. Times >7 minutes (420 seconds) indicate mobility limitations.
description: Assesses aerobic capacity and mobility through a 400-meter walking distance.
name: 400-Meter Walk Test

instructions: Sprint 15m, turn 180° at line, sprint back 5m. Time recorded for final 5m + turn + 5m return.
contraindications:
**Absolute Contraindications:**

- Individuals with acute musculoskeletal injuries, such as sprains or fractures, should avoid performing the 505 Agility Test to prevent exacerbation of injuries.

- Those with cardiovascular conditions, including uncontrolled hypertension or recent myocardial infarction, should refrain from high-intensity activities like the 505 Agility Test due to potential risks.

**Relative Contraindications:**

- Individuals with chronic conditions, such as osteoarthritis, may perform the test with caution, ensuring that the movements do not cause undue stress on affected joints.

- Athletes with a history of lower limb injuries should consult with a healthcare professional before attempting the test to assess readiness and prevent re-injury.

**Safety Considerations:**

- Ensure a clear, unobstructed area for testing to prevent tripping hazards.

- Use appropriate footwear to provide adequate support and traction during rapid direction changes.

- Warm-up thoroughly before testing to prepare muscles and joints for high-intensity movements.

- Monitor participants for signs of fatigue or distress during the test and provide adequate rest periods.
references: ([pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30781530/?utm_source=openai))
scoring_system: Time for 5m-turn-5m. <2.4s=excellent. Compare left vs right turning. Used for field sports.
description: 180-degree change of direction sprint test
name: 505 Agility Test

instructions:
Protocol: Walk 6 meters at normal pace; time middle 4 meters (allowing acceleration/deceleration zones).

Script: 'Walk at your usual pace from here to there.'
contraindications:
**Absolute Contraindications:**

- Recent myocardial infarction (within 5 days)

- Unstable angina during the previous month

- Resting heart rate >120 beats per minute

- Systolic blood pressure >180 mmHg or diastolic blood pressure >100 mmHg

- Oxygen saturation <85% at rest (unless under physician clearance)

- Acute illness (fever, infection)

- Uncontrolled arrhythmias

- Symptomatic aortic stenosis

- Acute pulmonary embolism or deep vein thrombosis

- Acute myocarditis or pericarditis

**Relative Contraindications:**

- Resting heart rate >110 beats per minute

- Systolic blood pressure >160 mmHg or diastolic blood pressure >90 mmHg

- Recent change in medications affecting exercise capacity

- Significant musculoskeletal limitations

- Poor understanding of instructions

**Safety Considerations:**

- Ensure continuous monitoring of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation during the test.

- Have emergency equipment readily available, including a crash cart and automated external defibrillator (AED).

- Provide clear instructions and ensure the participant understands the test procedure.

- Allow the participant to use their usual walking aids or prescribed oxygen during the test.

- Ensure the walking area is free from obstacles and has a clear path for the full six minutes.

**Red Flags:**

- Sudden onset of chest pain or discomfort

- Dizziness or lightheadedness

- Unusual shortness of breath

- Leg cramps or severe fatigue

- Palpitations or irregular heartbeats

- Any signs of distress or discomfort that may indicate a need to stop the test immediately.
references: Gait Speed Literature - Various normative studies.
scoring_system: Calculate speed in m/s. >1.0 m/s is normal in older adults. <0.8 m/s suggests mobility impairment.
description: Assesses preferred gait speed.
name: 6-Meter Walk Test

instructions: This test measures how your heart and lungs cope with continuous stepping. You'll step up and down on this step at a comfortable pace you feel you can maintain for six minutes. You can hold the rail if needed for balance. If at any time you feel chest pain, severe shortness of breath, dizziness, or anything that feels unsafe, please tell me immediately and we'll stop the test. You can stop at any time if you need to rest.
equipment_needed: Sturdy step of known and documented height; stopwatch or timer; optional handrail; heart rate monitor; sphygmomanometer or automated BP monitor; pulse oximeter; RPE scale.
contraindications: Do not perform the test in individuals with unstable angina, uncontrolled arrhythmias, recent myocardial infarction or surgery, uncontrolled severe hypertension, acute respiratory distress, resting SpO2 below protocol-specific safety thresholds (e.g., <88–90%), or where balance impairment makes stepping unsafe. Terminate the test if the client reports chest pain, intolerable dyspnoea, dizziness, near-syncope, severe leg pain, or if SpO2 or blood pressure move outside safe parameters.
references:
Mendes et al. – Six-Minute Step Test in Healthy Adults and Patients with Chronic Disease: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/
Rehabilitation Measures Database – 6-Minute Step Test: https://www.sralab.org/rehabilitation-measures/6-minute-step-test
scoring_system:
This is a functional capacity test rather than a pass/fail test. A higher total step count generally indicates better submaximal functional capacity. Clinicians may compare step count and physiological responses (HR, SpO2, symptoms) with normative data or previous tests to evaluate capacity and change over time. Abnormal responses include marked desaturation (e.g., SpO2 falling below clinically acceptable thresholds), excessive tachycardia, severe dyspnoea, chest pain, or other concerning symptoms.

Normative values for the 6-Minute Step Test vary based on step height, protocol, age, and clinical population. Published studies provide reference ranges for older adults, people with cardiac or pulmonary disease, and healthy individuals. Because protocols differ, clinicians should specify step height and reference which normative dataset is being used rather than applying a single normative table.
description: Functional aerobic capacity test measuring number of steps completed in 6 minutes
name: 6-Minute Step Test
6-Minute Walk Test
description: Measures the distance an individual can walk in 6 minutes on a flat, hard surface to evaluate functional exercise capacity and endurance.
instructions:
SETUP:
1. Mark 30-meter straight course with cones at each end
2. Prepare chair for rest if needed
3. Set up pulse oximeter and blood pressure cuff
4. Position stopwatch

PRE-TEST:
1. Measure baseline vitals: HR, BP, SpO2, RPE
2. Check for contraindications
3. Ensure client comfortable with equipment

STANDARDIZED INSTRUCTIONS (READ TO CLIENT):
"You are now going to do a 6 minute walk test. The object of this test is to walk as far as you can for six minutes so that you cover as much ground as possible.

You may slow down if necessary. If you stop, I want you to continue to walk again as soon as possible.

You will be regularly informed of the time and you will be encouraged to do your best. Your goal is to walk as far as possible in six minutes, but don't run or jog.

Please do not talk during the test unless you have a problem or I ask you a question. You must let me know if you have any chest pain or dizziness.

When the six minutes is up I will ask you to stop where you are. Do you have any questions?"

DURING TEST:
- Start timer when saying "Start walking now."
- Give standardized encouragement each minute:
  • 1 min: "You are doing well. You have 5 minutes to go."
  • 2 min: "Keep up the good work. You have 4 minutes to go."
  • 3 min: "You are doing well. You are halfway."
  • 4 min: "Keep up the good work. You have only 2 minutes left."
  • 5 min: "You are doing well. You have only 1 minute to go."
  • 6 min: "Please stop where you are."
- Count laps completed
- Record any rest periods with times and reasons
- Monitor for signs of distress

POST-TEST:
1. Stop client at exactly 6 minutes
2. Immediately measure: HR, SpO2, RPE, dyspnea (0-10 scale)
3. Calculate total distance (30m × complete laps + partial distance)
4. Allow recovery while seated
5. Record observations and notes
scoring_system:
Distance walked in meters. Compare to age, gender, height, and weight predicted values using equations:

Men: 6MWD(m) = (7.57 × height cm) - (5.02 × age) - (1.76 × weight kg) - 309m
Women: 6MWD(m) = (2.11 × height cm) - (2.29 × weight kg) - (5.78 × age) + 667m

Interpretation:
• <350m: Severe impairment
• 350-450m: Moderate impairment
• >450m: Mild/normal for most populations
• Healthy adults: typically 400-700m

Clinically meaningful difference: 30-50m change
equipment_needed: 30-meter hallway or track, measuring wheel or tape, cones (2), stopwatch, chair for rest, pulse oximeter, blood pressure cuff, clipboard with data sheet, Borg RPE scale
contraindications:
ABSOLUTE:
- Unstable angina in previous month
- Myocardial infarction within previous month
- Resting heart rate >120 bpm
- Systolic BP >180 mmHg
- Diastolic BP >100 mmHg

RELATIVE:
- Known moderate stenotic heart valve disease
- Electrolyte abnormalities
- Severe arterial hypertension (>200/110)
- Tachyarrhythmias or bradyarrhythmias
- Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
- High-degree atrioventricular block

STOP TEST IF:
- Chest pain or angina
- Intolerable dyspnea
- Leg cramps
- Staggering, lightheadedness, confusion
- Diaphoresis
- Pale or ashen appearance
- SpO2 drops below 80%
references:
ATS Committee on Proficiency Standards for Clinical Pulmonary Function Laboratories. ATS statement: guidelines for the six-minute walk test. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2002;166(1):111-117.

Enright PL, Sherrill DL. Reference equations for the six-minute walk in healthy adults. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 1998;158(5):1384-1387.

Casanova C, Celli BR, Barria P, et al. The 6-min walk distance in healthy subjects: reference standards from seven countries. Eur Respir J. 2011;37(1):150-156.

Polkey MI, Spruit MA, Edwards LD, et al. Six-minute-walk test in chronic obstructive pulmonary disease: minimal clinically important difference for death or hospitalization. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2013;187(4):382-386.

instructions:
Protocol: Complete as many stand-sit repetitions as possible in 60 seconds; arms crossed over chest.

Script: 'Stand and sit repeatedly for one minute.'
contraindications:
The 60-Second Sit-to-Stand Test (STS) is generally safe for most individuals; however, certain conditions may necessitate caution or exclusion from the test:

**Absolute Contraindications:**

- **Severe Cardiovascular Conditions:** Individuals with uncontrolled hypertension, recent myocardial infarction, or severe arrhythmias should avoid the test due to potential cardiovascular risks.

- **Acute Orthopedic Injuries:** Recent fractures, dislocations, or severe joint pain may be aggravated by the test.

- **Severe Respiratory Conditions:** Individuals with acute respiratory distress or severe chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) exacerbations may experience undue strain during the test.

**Relative Contraindications:**

- **Moderate Cardiovascular Conditions:** Individuals with well-controlled hypertension or stable cardiovascular conditions may perform the test with caution.

- **Musculoskeletal Limitations:** Those with chronic joint pain or mild osteoarthritis may participate, but modifications or assistance might be necessary.

**Safety Considerations:**

- **Supervision:** Conduct the test under the supervision of a trained professional to monitor for adverse reactions.

- **Pre-Test Screening:** Perform a thorough assessment to identify any potential risks before administering the test.

- **Emergency Preparedness:** Ensure immediate access to emergency medical services in case of adverse events.

Always consult with healthcare providers to determine the appropriateness of the 60-Second Sit-to-Stand Test for individual patients.

([pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37217083/?utm_source=openai))
references: ESSA Guidelines.
scoring_system: Count total repetitions. Lower normative values than 30-second STS due to longer duration.
description: Longer-duration sit-stand endurance assessment.
name: 60-Second Sit-to-Stand Test

instructions:
1. Place chair against wall and cone 8 feet (2.44m/96 inches) from front of chair
2. Participant sits in middle of chair, back straight, feet flat, hands on thighs
3. Demonstrate: on 'GO', stand up, walk quickly (but safely) around cone (to the right), return and sit down
4. Have participant practice once at 50% speed to ensure understanding
5. Ready position: hands on thighs, feet flat
6. Start timer on 'GO'
7. Participant completes full circuit
8. Stop timer when buttocks touch chair
9. Perform 2 test trials
10. Record each trial
11. Report best (faster) time
equipment_needed: Standard chair (43-45cm seat height), cone or marker, tape measure, stopwatch
contraindications: Severe balance impairment without assistive device, acute lower limb injury, unstable cardiovascular conditions
references:
Rikli RE, Jones CJ. Senior Fitness Test Manual. 2nd ed. Champaign, IL: Human Kinetics; 2013.

Jones CJ, Rikli RE, Beam WC. A 30-s chair-stand test as a measure of lower body strength in community-residing older adults. Res Q Exerc Sport. 1999;70(2):113-119.

Rikli RE, Jones CJ. Development and validation of criterion-referenced clinically relevant fitness standards for maintaining physical independence in later years. Gerontologist. 2013;53(2):255-267.

Jones CJ, Rikli RE. Measuring functional fitness of older adults. J Active Aging. 2002;1:24-30.
scoring_system: Time in seconds. Faster times indicate better agility and dynamic balance. Compare to age and gender norms from Senior Fitness Test. Higher times suggest increased fall risk and functional limitations.
description: Part of Senior Fitness Test measuring agility and dynamic balance. Participant stands from chair, walks around cone 8 feet (2.44m) away, and returns to sit.
name: 8-Foot Up-and-Go Test

instructions: Client rates confidence/emotions about returning to sport on 0-10 scale for 12 items.
contraindications:
Absolute contraindications:

- Severe cognitive impairments affecting comprehension and response accuracy.

- Acute psychiatric conditions that may influence self-reporting.

Relative contraindications:

- Recent significant psychological distress or trauma.

- Language barriers without appropriate translation support.

Safety considerations:

- Ensure a quiet, private environment to facilitate honest responses.

- Provide clear instructions and support for individuals with reading difficulties.

Red flags indicating the need for caution:

- Inconsistent or contradictory responses suggesting misunderstanding.

- Scores indicating high psychological distress that may require further evaluation.

Müller, A. M., & Künzler, D. (2025). The utility of psychological readiness scales in predicting return to sport: a systematic review. *BMC Psychology*, 13(1), 1-12. https://doi.org/10.1186/s40359-025-03378-5

Müller, A. M., & Künzler, D. (2025). The utility of psychological readiness scales in predicting return to sport: a systematic review. *BMC Psychology*, 13(1), 1-12. https://doi.org/10.1186/s40359-025-03378-5

Müller, A. M., & Künzler, D. (2025). The utility of psychological readiness scales in predicting return to sport: a systematic review. *BMC Psychology*, 13(1), 1-12. https://doi.org/10.1186/s40359-025-03378-5
scoring_system: Total 0-100. Higher = better psychological readiness. <56 suggests not ready to RTS. <76 predicts lower RTS rate.
description: 12-item scale measuring psychological readiness to return to sport after ACL injury
name: ACL-RSI

instructions: Client rates confidence (0-100%) in maintaining balance during 16 activities from easy (walking around house) to difficult (walking on icy sidewalks).
contraindications: The ABC Scale is a self-report measure and does not involve physical activity or exertion; therefore, there are no absolute or relative contraindications for its use. However, individuals with severe cognitive impairments or language barriers may find it challenging to accurately complete the scale. In such cases, assistance from a trained professional is recommended to ensure accurate assessment.

instructions: Patient section: rate difficulty 0-3 for 10 activities. Pain rated 0-10. Physician: assess ROM, strength, stability, signs.
contraindications:
Absolute Contraindications:

- Severe cognitive impairments that prevent understanding or completing the assessment.

- Acute shoulder injuries or conditions causing significant pain, which may affect the patient's ability to perform the assessment accurately.

Relative Contraindications:

- Recent shoulder surgeries or procedures that may influence the patient's current shoulder function.

- Severe pain or discomfort that may affect the patient's ability to perform the assessment accurately.

Safety Considerations:

- Ensure the patient is in a comfortable and safe environment to complete the assessment.

- Provide assistance if the patient has difficulty completing any part of the assessment.

Red Flags:

- If the patient reports severe pain or discomfort during the assessment, consider postponing the evaluation until the patient's condition stabilizes.

name: American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons (ASES) Score

instructions: Patient seated with knee flexed 90°. Stabilize tibia/fibula. Dorsiflex foot bringing toes toward shin. Measure angle between foot and tibia.
contraindications: Acute ankle injury, recent fracture, severe pain
references: ([pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22666642/?utm_source=openai)), ([doaj.org](https://doaj.org/article/ae9cd4facc8c4801aaf676aebe01e3d5?utm_source=openai)), ([pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25883868/?utm_source=openai)), ([pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21460462/?utm_source=openai)), ([pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23997389/?utm_source=openai))

name: Ankle Dorsiflexion ROM

instructions: Client supine, knee 90°, foot stabilized. Pull tibia anteriorly. Compare to opposite side.
contraindications:
*Absolute Contraindications*:

- Presence of open wounds over the knee joint.
- Signs of vascular compromise, such as diminished distal pulses.
- Gross deformity suggestive of fracture following major trauma.

*Relative Contraindications*:

- Severe joint effusion, which may increase intra-articular pressure and discomfort during the test.
- Acute hamstring spasm, which can affect the accuracy of the test.
- Recent knee dislocation; testing should be performed only after neurovascular stability is confirmed.

*Safety Considerations*:

- Ensure the patient is relaxed to minimize muscle tension that could affect test results.
- Compare findings with the contralateral knee to assess for asymmetry.
- Be cautious in patients with known knee pathologies that may affect test outcomes.

*Red Flags*:

- Inability to perform the test due to pain or muscle guarding.
- Signs of acute knee injury, such as significant swelling or instability.
references:
1. Crawford, S. N., & Spindler, K. P. (2010). Anterior cruciate ligament injuries: A review of the literature. *The Journal of Bone and Joint Surgery*, 92(6), 1381–1389. https://doi.org/10.2106/JBJS.I.01585
2. Hunt, K. J., & Krych, A. J. (2014). Anterior cruciate ligament injuries: Evaluation and management. *American Family Physician*, 89(12), 945–952. PMID: 24982589
3. Mok, K. M., & Chan, K. M. (2012). Clinical assessment of anterior cruciate ligament rupture. *Hong Kong Medical Journal*, 18(5), 396–402. PMID: 23143894

5. Koh, J. L., & McCarty, E. C. (2013). Anterior cruciate ligament injuries: Diagnosis and management. *American Family Physician*, 88(11), 741–748. PMID: 24274989
scoring_system: Positive = >5mm anterior translation compared to uninvolved side, soft end-feel. Less sensitive than Lachman.
description: Tests ACL integrity with knee at 90° flexion
name: Anterior Drawer Test (Knee)

instructions: Client prone, knee 90° flexion. Apply downward pressure on heel while rotating tibia internally and externally.
contraindications:
Absolute contraindications include:
- Gross knee deformities
- Obvious fractures or dislocations
- Recent knee surgeries

Relative contraindications include:
- Severe knee pain
- Swelling
- Inability to tolerate the test position

Safety considerations:
- Ensure proper stabilization to prevent injury
- Communicate with the patient to monitor discomfort

Red flags:
- Sudden increase in pain during the test
- Unusual joint sounds or movements
- Signs of vascular or neurological compromise
references:
1. Ockert B, et al. (2013). McMurray, JLT and Apley's tests shown to have poor diagnostic accuracy. *ACE Report*, 2(7):159. ([myorthoevidence.com](https://myorthoevidence.com/ace/738/mcmurray-jlt-and-apleys-tests-shown-to-have-poor-diagnostic-accuracy?utm_source=openai))

([pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26243431/?utm_source=openai))
3. Agresti D, et al. (2024). Apley Grind Test. *StatPearls*. ([statpearls.com](https://www.statpearls.com/nursepractitioner/ce/activity/22145?utm_source=openai))
scoring_system: Positive = pain with compression. Compare to Apley's distraction test. Pain with compression suggests meniscal pathology.
description: Tests meniscus by applying compression with rotation in prone position
name: Apley's Compression Test

instructions:
Self-report questionnaire. Ask client to tick the box that best describes their situation over the past week. 12 items across 4 domains. Takes 1-2 minutes to complete.

Script: 'I'm going to ask you 12 questions about how your health affects your daily life. Please answer based on how you have felt over the past week.'

Note: A utility score (0=death, 1=full health) can also be calculated using the AQoL algorithm available at www.aqol.com.au. Registration is required prior to using the AQoL in a research study.
references: Hawthorne G, Richardson J, Osborne R. The Assessment of Quality of Life (AQoL) instrument: a psychometric measure of health-related quality of life. Qual Life Res. 1999;8(3):209-224. Richardson J, Iezzi A, Khan MA, Maxwell A. Validity and reliability of the Assessment of Quality of Life (AQoL)-8D multi-attribute utility instrument. Patient. 2014;7(1):85-96.
scoring_system: Psychometric score: sum of all 12 items (0–3 each), range 0–36 (lower = better QoL). Domain scores: 0–9 per domain. Domains: Independent Living (Q1-3), Relationships (Q4-6), Senses (Q7-9), Mental Health (Q10-12).

questions.question_text: Do you need any help looking after yourself? (For example: dressing, bathing, eating)

options.label: I need no help at all.
Occasionally I need some help with personal care tasks.
I need help with the more difficult personal care tasks.
I need daily help with most or all personal care tasks.

questions.question_text: When doing household tasks: (For example: cooking, cleaning the house, washing)

options.label: I need no help at all.
Occasionally I need some help with household tasks.
I need help with the more difficult household tasks.
I need daily help with most or all household tasks.

questions.question_text: Thinking about how easily you can get around your home and community:

options.label: I get around my home and community by myself without any difficulty.
I find it difficult to get around my home and community by myself.
I cannot get around the community by myself, but I can get around my home with some difficulty.
I cannot get around either the community or my home by myself.

questions.question_text: Because of your health, your relationships (for example: with your friends, partner or parents) generally:

options.label: I have no close and warm relationships.

questions.question_text: Thinking about your relationship with other people:

options.label: I have plenty of friends, and am never lonely.
Although I have friends, I am occasionally lonely.
I have some friends, but am often lonely for company.
I am socially isolated and feel lonely.

questions.question_text: Thinking about your health and your relationship with your family:

options.label: My role in the family is unaffected by my health.
There are some parts of my family role I cannot carry out.
There are many parts of my family role I cannot carry out.
I cannot carry out any part of my family role.

questions.question_text: Thinking about your vision, including when using your glasses or contact lenses if needed:

options.label: I have some difficulty focusing on things, or I do not see them sharply.
I have a lot of difficulty seeing things. My vision is blurred.
I only see general shapes, or am blind.

questions.question_text: Thinking about your hearing, including using your hearing aid if needed:

options.label: I have some difficulty hearing or I do not hear clearly.
I have difficulty hearing things clearly. Often I do not understand what is said.
I hear very little indeed. I cannot fully understand loud voices speaking directly to me.

questions.question_text: When you communicate with others: (For example: by talking, listening, writing or signing.)

options.label: I have no trouble speaking to them or understanding what they are saying.
I have some difficulty being understood by people who do not know me.
I am only understood by people who know me well. I have great trouble understanding what others say.
I cannot adequately communicate with others.

questions.question_text: Thinking about how you sleep:

options.label: I am able to sleep without difficulty most of the time.
My sleep is interrupted some of the time, but I am usually able to go back to sleep without difficulty.
My sleep is interrupted most nights, but I am usually able to go back to sleep without difficulty.
I sleep in short bursts only. I am awake most of the night.

questions.question_text: Thinking about how you generally feel:

options.label: I do not feel anxious, worried or depressed.
I am slightly anxious, worried or depressed.
I feel moderately anxious, worried or depressed.

questions.question_text: How much pain or discomfort do you experience:

description: Health-related quality of life assessment.
name: Assessment of Quality of Life (AQoL)

instructions:
1. Participant stands in comfortable position
2. Demonstrate: reach one hand over shoulder down back, other hand up back from below
3. Try to overlap or touch middle fingers
4. Instruct preferred hand over shoulder first
5. Have participant practice once to determine better side
6. For test: reach both hands simultaneously
7. Measure distance between middle finger tips:
   - Overlap = positive score (measure overlap distance)
   - Gap = negative score (measure gap distance)
   - Touching = 0 cm
8. Perform 2 test trials on each side
9. Record best score for each side
10. Stop if pain occurs
contraindications: Recent shoulder surgery, acute rotator cuff injury, severe shoulder arthritis with pain, frozen shoulder
references: Rikli RE, Jones CJ. Senior Fitness Test Manual. 2nd ed. Champaign, IL: Human Kinetics; 2013.
scoring_system: Distance in centimeters. Zero = fingers touching, positive values = fingers overlap (better flexibility), negative values = fingers cannot touch (limited flexibility). Compare to age and gender norms.
description: Assesses shoulder flexibility and range of motion. Measures ability to reach behind back with both arms, relevant for daily activities like fastening bra or tucking in shirt.
name: Back Scratch Test

instructions: We're going to complete a detailed balance test called the BESTest. It looks at different parts of your balance system, including how you stand, move, and respond to pushes or changes in surfaces. I'll guide you through each task one at a time, explain or demonstrate what to do, and be close by to keep you safe. Some tasks may feel challenging, but you should stop and tell me if you feel unsteady, in pain, or unsafe in any way.
equipment_needed: Chair with and without armrests; firm surface and compliant (foam) surface; step or small platform; incline or ramp (if applicable); wall or support surface; stopwatch; measuring tape; gait belt; optional walkway and obstacles depending on the specific BESTest protocol version.
contraindications: Use heightened caution for clients with severe balance impairment, recent fractures or surgery, orthostatic hypotension, or conditions that make perturbations or walking tasks unsafe. Close guarding, a gait belt, and spotters are recommended in higher-risk clients. Terminate or modify tasks if the client appears at risk of falling, or if they experience dizziness, chest pain, sudden weakness, or other concerning symptoms.
references:
Horak et al. – The Balance Evaluation Systems Test (BESTest) to Differentiate Balance Deficits: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19796708/
Franchignoni et al. – The Mini-BESTest: A Shortened Version of the BESTest: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20460406/
Rehabilitation Measures Database – BESTest: https://www.sralab.org/rehabilitation-measures/balance-evaluation-systems-test-bestest
scoring_system:
Higher BESTest percentage scores indicate better balance and lower falls risk, while lower scores indicate greater balance impairment and higher risk. The BESTest can also identify which specific systems (domains) are most impaired, guiding targeted intervention (e.g., sensory integration vs biomechanical constraints vs anticipatory postural adjustments). Cut-offs for increased falls risk have been suggested in various populations (e.g., neurologic, older adults), typically based on total or section scores.

BESTest normative and cut-point data have been published for older adults and several clinical populations (e.g., Parkinson's disease, stroke). Some studies identify threshold scores associated with fall history or increased future falls risk. Because these thresholds vary by population, Base44 should allow clinicians to select relevant reference studies and record which cut-offs they are using, rather than embedding a single universal value.
description: Comprehensive 36-item balance evaluation identifying specific balance system deficits
name: Balance Evaluation Systems Test (BESTest)

instructions:
Protocol: Client walks on treadmill at constant speed (e.g., 3.3 mph). Grade increases at set intervals (e.g., 1% per minute). Test continues to volitional fatigue or clinical end-point. Time to exhaustion can be converted to VO2max via equations.

Script: 'You'll walk on the treadmill at a steady speed while the incline gradually increases. Tell me immediately if you feel chest pain, severe breathlessness, dizziness or want to stop.'
contraindications: High-risk cardiac clients without medical supervision, severe orthopedic limitations, uncontrolled hypertension
references:
ACSM Guidelines for Exercise Testing and Prescription.

Balke & Ware protocol.

ESSA Outcome Measures – Balke-Ware.
scoring_system: Record total test duration. ACSM and other sources provide VO2max prediction equations. Used with caution in clinical populations; modified protocols may be preferred.
description: Incremental treadmill protocol with constant speed and increasing grade, used to estimate or measure VO2max.
name: Balke-Ware Treadmill Test

instructions: Rate 10 ADL items (feeding, bathing, grooming, dressing, bowels, bladder, toilet use, transfers, mobility, stairs) based on actual performance.
contraindications:
The Barthel Index is a non-invasive assessment tool and is generally safe for all patients. However, caution should be exercised in the following situations:

- **Acute Medical Conditions**: Patients experiencing acute medical issues (e.g., severe infections, uncontrolled cardiovascular events) may not be suitable for assessment due to potential instability.

- **Severe Cognitive Impairment**: Individuals with significant cognitive deficits may not provide accurate self-reports, potentially affecting the reliability of the assessment.

- **Severe Pain or Discomfort**: Patients in severe pain may be unable to participate fully in the assessment, leading to incomplete or inaccurate results.

- **Non-Compliance or Refusal**: Patients who are unwilling or unable to cooperate with the assessment process may not yield valid results.

In such cases, alternative assessment methods or postponing the evaluation until the patient's condition stabilizes may be considered.

It's crucial to ensure that the assessment environment is safe and that the patient is comfortable to obtain accurate and reliable results.
references: ([pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17559756/?utm_source=openai))
scoring_system: Score 0-100 (or 0-20 in some versions). <60 suggests high dependency. >80 suggests functional independence.
description: 10-item assessment of activities of daily living independence
name: Barthel Index

instructions:
Protocol: Client responds to items rated on Likert scale (e.g., 0–4). Items grouped into subscales representing different regulation types (amotivation, external, introjected, identified, intrinsic). Score and interpret subscale means.

Script: 'This questionnaire asks about why you do, or don't do, exercise. Please rate how true each statement is for you.'
references:
Mullan, E., et al. – BREQ.

ESSA Outcome Measures – BREQ.
scoring_system: Calculate mean scores for each subscale. Higher autonomous regulation (intrinsic/identified) linked to better adherence; no single cut-off, interpret overall profile.
description: Measures types of exercise motivation along the self-determination continuum (amotivation to intrinsic motivation).
name: Behavioural Regulation in Exercise Questionnaire (BREQ)

instructions: Nine-point scoring system. One point for each positive manoeuvre: right and left little finger extension >90°, right and left thumb to forearm, right and left elbow hyperextension >10°, right and left knee hyperextension >10°, and forward flexion of trunk with knees straight and palms on floor.
contraindications: Acute joint injury, recent joint surgery
references: https://www.ehlers-danlos.org/information/beighton-score/

instructions:
SETUP:
1. Prepare a standard armchair with armrests, a chair without armrests, a stopwatch, a ruler, a step/stool (15cm high), and clear floor space
2. Position chairs against wall for safety
3. Ensure adequate space for all tasks

CLINICIAN SCRIPT TO CLIENT:
"This test will assess your balance in different positions and activities. I'll ask you to perform 14 different tasks. For each one, I'll explain what to do and give you a score based on how well you can do it. Some tasks may be challenging - that's expected. Your safety is most important, so I'll stay close to you throughout. Do you have any questions before we begin?"

TESTING PROCEDURE:
1. Demonstrate each task as needed
2. Allow one practice attempt if requested
3. Stay within arm's reach for safety
4. Score each item 0-4 based on performance
5. Stop test if client becomes unsafe
6. Record scores immediately

ITEMS TO ASSESS:
1. SITTING TO STANDING: "Please stand up. Try not to use your hands for support."
2. STANDING UNSUPPORTED: "Please stand for two minutes without holding on."
3. SITTING UNSUPPORTED: "Please sit with arms folded for 2 minutes."
4. STANDING TO SITTING: "Please sit down."
5. TRANSFERS: "I'd like you to move from this chair to that chair and back."
6. STANDING WITH EYES CLOSED: "Please close your eyes and stand still for 10 seconds."
7. STANDING WITH FEET TOGETHER: "Place your feet together and stand without holding on for 1 minute."
8. REACHING FORWARD: "Lift your arm to 90 degrees and reach forward as far as you can."
9. PICK UP OBJECT: "Pick up this object from the floor."
10. TURNING TO LOOK BEHIND: "Turn and look directly behind you over your left shoulder, then your right."
11. TURN 360 DEGREES: "Turn completely around in a full circle, then turn in the other direction."
12. PLACING FEET ON STOOL: "Place each foot alternately on this step 4 times."
13. TANDEM STANDING: "Place one foot directly in front of the other."
14. STANDING ON ONE LEG: "Stand on one leg as long as you can."

SCORING FOR EACH ITEM:
4 = Able to perform task safely and independently
3 = Able to perform with supervision or extra time
2 = Able to perform with minimal assistance or modifications
1 = Able to perform with assistance
0 = Unable to perform safely
equipment_needed: Standard armchair with armrests, chair without armrests, stopwatch, ruler (30cm), step/stool (15cm high), small object (shoe/slipper), clear floor space
contraindications: Severe cognitive impairment, inability to stand independently for 2+ minutes, acute medical conditions affecting balance
references:
Berg KO, Wood-Dauphinee SL, Williams JI, Maki B. Measuring balance in the elderly: validation of an instrument. Can J Public Health. 1992;83 Suppl 2:S7-11.

Shumway-Cook A, Baldwin M, Polissar NL, Gruber W. Predicting the probability for falls in community-dwelling older adults. Phys Ther. 1997;77(8):812-819.

Bogle Thorbahn LD, Newton RA. Use of the Berg Balance Test to predict falls in elderly persons. Phys Ther. 1996;76(6):576-585.

Steffen TM, Hacker TA, Mollinger L. Age- and gender-related test performance in community-dwelling elderly people: Six-Minute Walk Test, Berg Balance Scale, Timed Up & Go Test, and gait speeds. Phys Ther. 2002;82(2):128-137.
scoring_system: Each task scored 0-4 points. Total score: 0-20 = High fall risk, 21-40 = Medium fall risk, 41-56 = Low fall risk
description: A 14-item scale designed to measure balance in older adults. The Berg Balance Scale is widely used in clinical practice to assess static and dynamic balance abilities.
name: Berg Balance Scale

instructions: Client fasted, euhydrated. Lie supine. Electrodes placed on hand and foot. Small current passed through body. Measures resistance and reactance.
contraindications:
Absolute contraindications for BIA include:

- Presence of implanted medical devices such as pacemakers or defibrillators.

- Pregnancy, due to potential risks to fetal development.

Relative contraindications include:

- Individuals with significant fluid and electrolyte imbalances, as these can affect the accuracy of BIA measurements.

- Patients with severe edema or dehydration, which can alter body water distribution and impact results.

Safety considerations involve:

- Ensuring that the electrical current used is of low intensity and safe for human exposure.

- Standardizing measurement protocols to minimize variability and ensure consistent results.

Red flags indicating when not to perform BIA include:

- Recent consumption of large amounts of food or beverages, which can temporarily alter body composition.

- Recent intense physical activity, which can affect hydration status and body water distribution.

- Ambient temperature extremes, as they can influence body water distribution and resistance measurements.
references:
1. Deurenberg, P., Deurenberg-Yap, M., & Guricci, S. (2002). Body mass index and percent body fat: a meta analysis among different ethnic groups. International Journal of Obesity, 26(6), 716-728.

2. Kyle, U. G., Bosaeus, I., De Lorenzo, A. D., Deurenberg, P., Elia, M., Gómez, J. M., ... & Pirlich, M. (2004). Bioelectrical impedance analysis—part I: review of principles and methods. Clinical Nutrition, 23(5), 1226-1243.

3. Lukaski, H. C., & Bolonchuk, W. W. (1988). Assessment of fat-free mass using bioelectrical impedance measurements of human body composition. The American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 48(4), 935-939.

4. Kyle, U. G., Bosaeus, I., De Lorenzo, A. D., Deurenberg, P., Elia, M., Gómez, J. M., ... & Pirlich, M. (2004). Bioelectrical impedance analysis—part II: utilization in clinical practice. Clinical Nutrition, 23(6), 1430-1453.

5. Lukaski, H. C., & Bolonchuk, W. W. (1988). Assessment of fat-free mass using bioelectrical impedance measurements of human body composition. The American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 48(4), 935-939.
scoring_system: Estimates body fat %, fat-free mass, total body water. Accuracy affected by hydration. Less accurate than DEXA but portable/quick.
description: Estimates body composition by measuring electrical resistance through body
name: Bioelectrical Impedance Analysis (BIA)
Body Fat Percentage (Skinfolds)
description: Estimates body fat % using sum of skinfold thicknesses. Useful for tracking changes in body composition, particularly in fitness settings.
instructions:
Protocol:
1. Identify skinfold sites (ESSA 4-site or 7-site protocol).
2. Grasp skinfold firmly between thumb and index finger.
3. Place caliper 1 cm below fingers, perpendicular to fold.
4. Maintain grasp and read measurement at 2 seconds.
5. Record to nearest 0.1 mm.
6. Repeat each site 2–3 times; use median value.
7. Calculate body fat % using ESSA or Jackson–Pollock equations.

Script: 'I'll take a series of skinfold measurements to estimate your body fat percentage. This requires light contact with the skin. Tell me if any measurement feels uncomfortable.'

Common Sites (ESSA 4-site):
• Biceps
• Triceps
• Subscapular
• Suprailiac

7-site protocol adds:
• Chest
• Midaxillary
• Abdominal
scoring_system: Record skinfold thickness to nearest 0.1 mm for each site. Calculate sum of skinfolds and estimate body fat percentage using validated equations (Jackson-Pollock or ESSA protocols). Compare to age and sex-specific normative charts. Track longitudinal changes for best interpretation.
equipment_needed: Accredited skinfold calipers (e.g., Harpenden, Lange), skin-safe marker, measurement datasheet
contraindications: Trained assessors only. Avoid in clients with obesity (difficult to obtain valid measurements), frail skin, bruising disorders, or those uncomfortable with skin contact.
references:
Jackson, A. S., & Pollock, M. L. (1978). Generalized equations for predicting body density of men. British Journal of Nutrition, 40(3), 497-504.

ESSA Skinfold Measurement Protocol.

ACSM's Guidelines for Exercise Testing and Prescription - Body Composition Assessment.

name: Body Mass Index (BMI)
description: Body Mass Index estimates weight relative to height and categorises health risk related to body mass.
instructions:
Protocol:
1. Obtain height (m) and weight (kg).
2. Calculate BMI = weight_kg / (height_m²).

Script: 'I'm going to calculate your BMI using your height and weight. BMI is only one indicator of health and may not represent muscle or fluid differences.'

WHO Classifications:
• Underweight: <18.5 kg/m²
• Healthy weight: 18.5–24.9 kg/m²
• Overweight: 25–29.9 kg/m²
• Obesity: ≥30 kg/m²
scoring_system: BMI = weight (kg) / height (m)². Auto-classify into WHO category based on result: Underweight (<18.5), Healthy weight (18.5-24.9), Overweight (25-29.9), Obese (≥30).
contraindications: Not suitable as a standalone diagnostic tool. Interpret with caution in muscular individuals, older adults, and those with oedema.
references:
WHO (2006). BMI Classification.

ESSA Outcome Measures Book – BMI Section.

instructions:
1. **Preparation**: Ensure the participant is comfortable and understands the purpose of the Borg Rating of Perceived Exertion (RPE) scale, which measures the intensity of physical activity based on personal perception.

2. **Introduction to the Scale**: Explain that the scale ranges from 6 to 20, where 6 represents 'no exertion at all' and 20 signifies 'maximal exertion'. ([med.umich.edu](https://www.med.umich.edu/1libr/Stroke/BorgScale?utm_source=openai))

3. **During Activity**: While the participant engages in the physical activity, periodically ask them to rate their perceived exertion using the scale. Encourage honest self-assessment to accurately gauge exercise intensity.

4. **Post-Activity Assessment**: After completing the activity, request a final RPE rating to evaluate the overall intensity experienced.

5. **Documentation**: Record the RPE scores at each interval and the final score for analysis.

6. **Feedback**: Discuss the ratings with the participant to understand their perception of the activity's intensity and adjust future exercise plans accordingly.

**Note**: Familiarizing the participant with the scale before the activity can enhance the accuracy of their assessments.
equipment_needed: No specialized equipment is required to administer the Borg RPE scale. A printed or digital version of the scale for reference is recommended.
contraindications: The Borg RPE scale is a subjective measure and may not be suitable for individuals who have difficulty self-assessing their exertion levels due to cognitive impairments or language barriers. Additionally, it may not be appropriate for those with severe cardiovascular or respiratory conditions where precise monitoring of physiological parameters is essential.
references: Cleveland Clinic – Borg Rating of Perceived Exertion Scale: https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/articles/17450-borg-rating-of-perceived-exertion-rpe-scale - The Borg RPE scale ranges from 6 (no exertion) to 20 (maximal exertion).

The original scale ranges from 6 (no exertion) to 20 (maximal exertion). Moderate-intensity activity corresponds to ratings of 12–14. A modified CR10 scale runs from 0 (nothing at all) to 10 (very, very heavy). Participants consider factors such as breathing rate, heart rate, muscle fatigue and sweat when selecting a number.

instructions:
Protocol: Move wooden blocks one at a time across partition for 60 seconds. Count blocks transferred.

Script: 'Move as many blocks as possible in one minute.'
contraindications:
The Box and Block Test is generally safe for most individuals. However, certain conditions may necessitate caution or avoidance:

**Absolute Contraindications:**

- **Severe Upper Limb Impairment:** Individuals with significant upper limb disabilities or amputations may find the test challenging or unfeasible.

- **Acute Musculoskeletal Injuries:** Recent fractures, sprains, or dislocations of the upper limb should be fully healed before attempting the test.

**Relative Contraindications:**

- **Severe Pain:** Individuals experiencing acute or chronic pain in the upper limb may find the test uncomfortable.

- **Severe Spasticity or Rigidity:** Conditions like advanced Parkinson's disease or severe cerebral palsy may affect the ability to perform the test.

**Safety Considerations:**

- **Supervision:** Ensure that a trained professional supervises the test to assist in case of sudden discomfort or injury.

- **Environment:** Conduct the test in a safe, clutter-free area to prevent tripping or other accidents.

- **Warm-Up:** Encourage participants to perform gentle warm-up exercises to prepare the upper limbs for activity.

**Red Flags:**

- **Sudden Onset of Pain:** If the participant experiences sharp or sudden pain during the test, discontinue immediately.

- **Dizziness or Nausea:** Any signs of dizziness, lightheadedness, or nausea should prompt immediate cessation of the test.

- **Unusual Fatigue:** Excessive fatigue or signs of distress are indicators to stop the assessment.

Always consult with a healthcare professional before administering the BBT to ensure it is appropriate for the individual's health status.
references: Mathiowetz, V., et al. (1985). Adult norms for the Box and Block Test of manual dexterity.
scoring_system: Count blocks moved. Compare to age/sex normative data.
description: Gross manual dexterity assessment.
name: Box and Block Test

instructions: Ask client to rate pain severity (worst, least, average, current) on 0-10 scales, and rate how pain interferes with 7 daily activities (general activity, mood, walking, work, relations, sleep, enjoyment).
equipment_needed: No physical equipment is required to administer the BPI. It is a paper-and-pencil questionnaire that can also be administered electronically.
contraindications: The BPI is a non-invasive, self-report tool and is generally safe for all patients. However, it may not be suitable for individuals who are unable to understand or respond to the questions due to cognitive impairments, severe language barriers, or acute distress. In such cases, alternative assessment methods should be considered.
references:
Cleeland, C. S., & Ryan, K. M. (1994). Pain assessment: global use of the Brief Pain Inventory. *Annals of the Academy of Medicine, Singapore*, 23(2), 129-138. 
Cleeland, C. S., & Ryan, K. M. (1994). Pain assessment: global use of the Brief Pain Inventory. *Annals of the Academy of Medicine, Singapore*, 23(2), 129-138. 
Cleeland, C. S., & Ryan, K. M. (1994). Pain assessment: global use of the Brief Pain Inventory. *Annals of the Academy of Medicine, Singapore*, 23(2), 129-138.
scoring_system: Pain Severity: Average of 4 items (0-10). Pain Interference: Average of 7 items (0-10). Scores: 1-4 = Mild, 5-6 = Moderate, 7-10 = Severe pain/interference.

description: Assesses pain severity and pain interference with daily living. Widely used in chronic pain management to evaluate both pain intensity and its impact on function.

instructions: 7 stages, each 3 minutes. Speed and grade increase each stage. Stage 1: 1.7mph/10%. Continue to volitional exhaustion.
equipment_needed: Treadmill, heart rate monitor, BP cuff
contraindications:
**Absolute Contraindications:**

- Acute myocardial infarction within the past 2 days
- Unstable angina
- Uncontrolled arrhythmias causing symptoms
- Symptomatic severe aortic stenosis
- Uncontrolled heart failure
- Acute pulmonary embolism or pulmonary infarction
- Acute myocarditis or pericarditis
- Acute aortic dissection

**Relative Contraindications:**

- Left main coronary stenosis
- Moderate stenotic valvular heart disease
- Electrolyte abnormalities
- Severe arterial hypertension (systolic > 200 mmHg or diastolic > 110 mmHg)
- Tachyarrhythmias or bradyarrhythmias
- Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and other forms of outflow tract obstruction
- Mental impairment with limited ability to cooperate
- High-degree atrioventricular block

**Safety Considerations:**

- Ensure the presence of trained medical personnel during testing
- Continuous monitoring of ECG, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation
- Availability of emergency equipment and medications
- Informed consent obtained from the participant

**Red Flags:**

- Chest pain or discomfort
- Dizziness or near-syncope
- Shortness of breath disproportionate to the workload
- Leg cramps or claudication
- Pale or cold skin
- Nausea or vomiting
- Severe fatigue or exhaustion

**Note:** The decision to perform the Bruce Treadmill Protocol should be individualized, considering the patient's medical history, current health status, and the purpose of the assessment.
references:
1. Cumming, G. R., & McDonald, R. (1978). Bruce treadmill test in children: Normal values in a clinic population. *The American Journal of Cardiology*, 41(1), 69–75. https://doi.org/10.1016/0002-9149(78)90134-0

2. van der Cammen-van Zijp, M. H., et al. (2009). Exercise testing of pre-school children using the Bruce treadmill protocol: new reference values. *European Journal of Applied Physiology*, 106(5), 735–741. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00421-009-1236-x

3. van der Cammen-van Zijp, M. H., et al. (2009). Exercise capacity in Dutch children: new reference values for the Bruce treadmill protocol. *European Journal of Applied Physiology*, 106(5), 723–733. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00421-009-1235-y

4. Yalcin, S., et al. (1998). Bruce treadmill test in healthy Turkish children: endurance time, heart rate, blood pressure and electrocardiographic changes. *Acta Cardiologica*, 53(6), 357–363. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/9677721/

5. van der Cammen-van Zijp, M. H., et al. (2009). Exercise testing of pre-school children using the Bruce treadmill protocol: new reference values. *European Journal of Applied Physiology*, 106(5), 735–741. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00421-009-1236-x
scoring_system: VO2max estimated from time or stage completed. Time-based formula: VO2max = 2.94 + (0.65 × test time min) - (0.026 × age) + 6.47 (if female).
description: Most common maximal treadmill protocol with 3-minute stages of increasing speed/grade
name: Bruce Treadmill Protocol

instructions: Client rates 8 items (cough, phlegm, chest tightness, breathlessness, activities, confidence, sleep, energy) on 0-5 scale.
contraindications:
The COPD Assessment Test (CAT) is a self-administered questionnaire designed to assess the health-related quality of life in individuals with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). As a non-invasive tool, it has no absolute contraindications. However, relative contraindications include:

- **Severe Cognitive Impairment**: Individuals with significant cognitive deficits may find it challenging to comprehend or accurately respond to the questionnaire items, potentially leading to unreliable results.

- **Severe Visual Impairment**: Those with profound visual impairments might struggle to read and interpret the questions, affecting the validity of their responses.

- **Language Barriers**: Patients who do not understand the language in which the CAT is administered may provide inaccurate answers, compromising the assessment's effectiveness.

**Safety Considerations**:

- **Assisted Administration**: For individuals with cognitive or visual impairments, it is advisable to have a trained healthcare professional assist in administering the CAT to ensure accurate responses.

- **Cultural Sensitivity**: Ensure that the version of the CAT used is culturally appropriate and has been validated for the specific population to avoid misinterpretation of questions.

- **Regular Review**: Healthcare providers should periodically review the CAT results in conjunction with other clinical assessments to make informed decisions about patient care.
references: ([pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24957783/?utm_source=openai))
scoring_system: Total 0-40. <10=low impact, 10-20=medium, 21-30=high, >30=very high. MCID=2 points.
